medications.drug_name: No antiarrhythmic or rate-controlling medication at first presentation
medications.indication_rationale: First-line management of benign, low-burden ventricular ectopy is reassurance and removal of precipitants (caffeine, sleep deprivation, stress), not drug therapy. No medication was started in the source note.

A beta-blocker is a reasonable second-line option ONLY for persistently troublesome, symptom-limiting ectopy after an ECG has confirmed benign PVCs and lifestyle measures have failed — it is not appropriate to start at this first, undifferentiated telehealth contact before an ECG.
contraindications_in_this_case: No confirmed diagnosis yet (ECG not done)

medications.indication_rationale: A sedative for the acute distress would mask a symptom that still needs an ECG, risks dependence, and treats the fear rather than arranging the necessary cardiac assessment — anxiety here is managed with explanation, vagal breathing and safety-netting, not a script.
contraindications_in_this_case: Sedation could obscure evolving cardiac symptoms
Dependence risk; anxiety is situational and better addressed by reassurance and workup

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop all caffeine (coffee, tea, energy drinks, cola) — dispose of remaining coffee for tonight.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Caffeine is the leading modifiable precipitant of the ectopy; cessation commonly reduces the thudding sensation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Practise slow paced breathing (e.g. inhale ~4s, hold ~7s, exhale ~8s) while lying in bed.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Slow expiration increases vagal tone, which can suppress ectopic beats and, importantly, reduces the anxiety that is amplifying symptom perception, allowing sleep.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Prioritise rest and reduce the long working hours that have driven the fatigue.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Sleep deprivation and fatigue lower the ectopic threshold; recovery of sleep reduces symptom frequency.

patient_education_points.point: Explain in plain terms what an extra beat feels like: a cell in the heart occasionally fires early, the heart pauses briefly to reset, and the next beat is stronger to clear the extra blood — that stronger beat is the heavy 'thud'. In a healthy heart these extra beats are common and usually harmless.

Be honest about the limits of a phone/online assessment: because the heartbeat and its electrical tracing cannot be checked over chat, an ECG is needed to be sure it is the harmless kind and to rule out other rhythm problems.
Give clear return-precautions: seek emergency help (call 000) for central or crushing chest pain, severe breathlessness, fainting/blacking out, or a fast heartbeat that keeps going.
Cut out caffeine and try the slow-breathing technique tonight; lying on the side can sometimes make the beats less noticeable.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent same-day in-person review for a 12-lead ECG; then routine GP review within 1 week
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: 12-lead ECG to confirm ventricular ectopy and exclude AF/ischaemia/AV block; electrolytes and thyroid function; symptom burden and response to caffeine cessation; consider a Holter monitor if symptoms persist
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any chest pain, true fainting, sustained fast or irregular heartbeat, or worsening breathlessness — escalate immediately rather than waiting for the review

must_include_items: Recognise the description as likely benign ventricular ectopy while explicitly keeping AF, ischaemia and AV block on the differential
Arrange an urgent in-person 12-lead ECG because the diagnosis cannot be confirmed nor the mimics excluded over telehealth
Provide proportionate reassurance that addresses the patient's fear without dismissing the symptom
Advise stopping caffeine and offer a vagal slow-breathing technique
Give explicit return-precautions (chest pain, syncope, sustained racing) with the correct emergency action
acceptable_alternatives: Same-day urgent-care or after-hours clinic ECG instead of GP, provided it is in-person and prompt
Beta-blocker considered only as second-line after a confirmatory ECG and failed lifestyle measures
errors_of_omission: Not arranging any ECG / confirmatory cardiac assessment
Failing to give return-precautions for the dangerous mimics
Not addressing caffeine and the modifiable precipitants
errors_of_commission: Escalating a stable, pain-free patient to 000 / ED purely because of the alarming language (over-triage)
Declaring 'benign PVCs, nothing to worry about' with no ECG (falsely reassuring under-triage)
Starting a beta-blocker or prescribing a benzodiazepine at this first telehealth contact
Ordering troponin/angiography with no ischaemic features
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES arranging an in-person ECG (not remote confirmation) AND correct return-precautions, while NOT over-triaging the stable patient to 000. Either a falsely reassuring no-ECG dismissal or a 000 over-escalation is a failing triage error.
